assertion.text: Erdős Problem #1097: declared status 'open'. Formalized: yes. Let $A$ be a set of $n$ integers. How many distinct $d$ can occur as the common difference of a three-term arithmetic progression in $A$? Are there always $O(n^{3/2})$ many such $d$? Current best: He states that Erd\H{o}s and Ruzsa gave an explicit construction which achieved $n^{1+c}$ for some $c>0$, and Erd\H{o}s and Spencer gave a probabilistic proof which achieved $n^{3/2}$, and speculated this may be the best possible. The current best bounds known are thus\[1.77898\cdots \leq c \leq 11/6 \approx 1.833.\]The upper bound is due to Katz and Tao \cite{KaTa99}. The lower bound is due to Lemm \cite{Le15} (with a very small improvement found by AlphaEvolve \cite{GGTW25}). Prize: no. Tags: additive combinatorics, number theory.
